feed: I love games — show me great new release

cards.title: The gamedev sim where the publisher screws you
cards.caption: A dev-life sim where crowdfunding fails and publishers are shady. The most honest gamedev tutorial is a game — simulators teach market mechanics faster than postmortems, because you pay the tuition in save files.

cards.title: Jonathan Blow spent ten years on one game
cards.caption: Blow on whether indie is broken, a decade into one project. Indie's edge was never taste — it was iteration speed. When auteurs adopt AAA timelines they inherit AAA risk with none of the hedges.

cards.title: Demos are back as indie marketing's default
cards.caption: Next week's indie slate ships with demos attached. A demo is a refund-proof trial — Steam's 2-hour window made short games returnable anyway, so giving away hour one costs nothing and filters refunders. Demo lists are half my weekend queue.

cards.title: Paid mobile games up 77% — the F2P math broke
cards.caption: Premium mobile releases jumped 77% in 2025. Not nostalgia — arithmetic: when acquiring whales costs more than their LTV, charging everyone $8 upfront wins. The pendulum is priced, not cultural. Caught it in my gaming lane before the trades.

cards.title: One dev, a poker roguelike, five Game Awards nods
cards.caption: Balatro is up for GOTY against nine-figure budgets. The market pays for a novel mechanic, not production value — one new game loop beats a thousand artists polishing a known one. Budgets hedge; they don't invent.

cards.title: Quality isn't the bottleneck. Discovery is
cards.caption: A reviewer betting on games 'easily overlooked' in the release flood. With ~19k Steam launches a year, visibility is the scarce resource — good games fail at distribution, not craft. Which is basically why I outsourced my scroll.

cards.title: Pick the engine by how little time you have
cards.caption: A dev going back to Godot because the project doesn't deserve much time. Scoping tools to ambition is the senior move — engine choice is a time budget, not a loyalty. Most abandoned games died of over-tooling, not lack of talent.

cards.title: Nintendo's moat is 30-year careers, not IP
cards.caption: DK Bananza reads as veterans mentoring novices — and that's the tell. Craft compounds only where devs aren't laid off every cycle. Nintendo's real advantage is retention: institutional knowledge the rest of the industry keeps torching.
cards.quote: It's clearly the work of lifelong game developers mentoring novices

cards.title: Steam reviews are a bug tracker with leverage
cards.caption: Players file demo nitpicks as negative reviews because reviews are the one channel devs visibly answer. Not malice — leverage. Every other feedback path is a suggestion box; the review is the only one that moves the score.

cards.title: One outlier review can decide an indie launch
cards.caption: A solo dev on shipping under Metacritic's rule. Aggregators are lossy compression — and small games have tiny samples, so one outlier review moves the score like a whale in a thin order book. Big games get averaged; small ones get decided.
cards.quote: it feels like we're all living under the rule of Metacritic

cards.title: Maturity in gamedev: bullet points over PowerPoint
cards.caption: Process weight is a tax on iteration, and iteration is the only edge a small team has. Every artifact polished for stakeholders is a build you didn't ship. Pulled from the gamedev fediverse, where the good one-liners live.
cards.quote: Maturity in gamedev means favoring bullet points over PowerPoint.

cards.title: A solo dev's birthday wish: food icons
cards.caption: The joke is the economics: for a solo dev, code is free evenings but art is cash. Asset scarcity, not programming, is the real bottleneck — which is why icon packs are a market and the engines are free.

cards.title: Good sim engines don't know what beer is
cards.caption: A devlog rule worth stealing: keep the engine ignorant. The moment your engine 'knows' a domain concept you've hard-coded the design space — beer as pure data is what lets next month's whiskey ship without a refactor.
cards.quote: The Engine Doesn't Know What Beer Is

cards.title: 'I was following a spreadsheet' — how design dies
cards.caption: Ex-Rocksteady leads on Suicide Squad: design steered by commercial metrics. Metrics optimize proxies, and players buy what proxies can't see — the harder you chase replayability numbers, the faster the fun leaks out. Goodhart, playable.
cards.quote: I started feeling like I wasn't making games anymore. I was following a spreadsheet

cards.title: 55,000 refunds for finishing the game too fast
cards.caption: Solo dev ships a complete sub-2-hour game; players finish it and refund inside Steam's window. Refund rules are game design pressure nobody votes on — they tax short games and subsidize padding. Surfaced a day before the discourse.

cards.title: Godot ships previews while Unity ships apologies
cards.caption: Godot 4.8 Dev1 is out. The cadence is the story: an open engine iterating in public while Unity rebuilds trust means the default for new indies already flipped. Engines are won by momentum, not feature lists.

cards.title: Digital pays studios 2x. Physical never had a chance
cards.caption: Lords of the Fallen's CEO: studios earn twice as much from digital sales. Every 'players prefer digital' narrative is a margin story wearing a demand costume — the storefront didn't win your heart, it won the P&L.

cards.title: A hit built on stock assets proves the point
cards.caption: Kenney cheering a hit game that ships with his asset packs. Asset packs are gamedev's open-source libraries — reuse isn't cutting corners, it's comparative advantage. Design is the scarce input; art was always importable.

cards.title: 'Resetting Xbox' is the console business admitting it
cards.caption: Xbox's own blog calls it a reset. Read it as an exit: hardware becomes a Game Pass endpoint, and 'console' shifts from business model to distribution layer. The generation war ends not with a winner but with a subscription.

cards.title: AAA was a financing tier, not a quality tier
cards.caption: Players never bought budgets — the AAA/AA/indie split was investor vocabulary that leaked into reviews. When release volume guarantees supply at every price point, the label stops predicting anything a player can actually feel.

cards.title: The Dreamcast still gets new games. On purpose
cards.caption: Dead platforms are great targets: frozen hardware, no cert process, and collectors who pre-order everything. A 26-year-old console with a healthier physical scene than PS5 is niche economics, not nostalgia.

cards.title: Studios close whether the game sells or not
cards.caption: When outcomes stop correlating with sales, sales were never the variable. Studios are balance-sheet items now — closures get triggered by acquisition accounting and interest rates, not by whether anyone bought the game.
cards.quote: Game sells good - Studio gone. Game sells bad - Studio gone.

cards.title: 96% of indie games make no money — by design
cards.caption: That failure rate isn't the market breaking; it's gatekeeping's removal made visible. Publishers used to reject the slush pile in private — Steam publishes it. The median game always earned zero; now there's just a chart.

cards.title: Physical games are merch now — and that's a market
cards.caption: A dev who shipped a physical indie run, on PlayStation's retreat: discs stopped being distribution years ago. The buyers are collectors, not players — physical is a merch-margin business, and Sony just handed it to the boutiques.

cards.title: Sponsored gamedev-advice compilation video
New devs aren't choosing Godot. They're fleeing Unity.
cards.caption: Every '1 year of gamedev, no experience' video is a Godot video now. That's not preference — it's Unity's runtime-fee trust collapse compounding. Engines lose beginners first and revenue five years later.

cards.title: Review scores were always price-adjusted
cards.caption: The art-vs-product debate ends the moment price enters the score. Critics have quietly graded value-per-pound forever — a £70 tag just makes the deduction visible. Score the game, invoice separately.
cards.quote: no game that costs over sixty quid is more than a 7/10

cards.title: 'Build it and they will come' is survivorship bias
cards.caption: Comforting advice for indie devs — from the third of Steam games that ever cleared $1,000. Discovery isn't a reward for quality; it's a second product you ship or you don't. The graveyard is full of kick-ass games nobody found.
cards.quote: If you build it, they will come.

cards.title: 65.9% of Steam games gross under $1,000
cards.caption: The indie boom sold more lottery tickets, not more prizes. Median outcome on Steam is a hobby with an invoice attached. The real dividing line isn't quality — it's whether anyone was ever told the game exists.

cards.title: Steam made ownership obsolete by being reliable
cards.caption: Nobody misses discs because Valve never gave them a reason to. Trust turned out to be a substitute for property rights — which means the whole ownership debate is one bad outage away from reopening.

cards.title: Daily indie release roundup
Scoreboard animation devlog, nothing non-obvious
Dutch polders are an indie moat
cards.caption: A driving game through Dutch dikes and windmills, built in public. Hyper-local settings are terrain AAA can't touch — nobody greenlights the Netherlands. Specificity is the cheapest moat left. A tuned feed catches these at prototype stage.

cards.title: 10 years of free games: 99k views, donation income
cards.caption: A decade of shipped games earns roughly one viral tweet's worth of attention. itch.io isn't a market, it's patronage — and knowing which economy you're actually in beats pretending they're the same one.

cards.title: Skill tree pixel-art clip, no take available
Asset reuse is a design-risk budget
cards.caption: Majora's Mask exists because Ocarina's assets were already paid for. Reuse isn't laziness — it's amortizing production so the weird ideas get funded. Teams that rebuild everything spend their risk budget on rocks and doors.
cards.quote: developers reusing game assets is a good thing, actually

cards.title: Game AI didn't get dumber. It got quieter.
cards.caption: F.E.A.R.'s soldiers felt brilliant because they announced their flanks out loud. Perceived intelligence is a communication problem — photoreal enemies stopped narrating, so their smarts turned invisible. Then studios cut them.

cards.title: Game announcement, pure promo
A writer locked out of her own first game
cards.caption: Mobile is the first medium where authors lose access to their own back catalog. No manuscripts, no reprints — when the server dies, the work never existed. Preservation isn't nostalgia, it's version control for an art form.
cards.quote: it was released 12 years ago and you can't play it anymore

cards.title: Genre is a search index, not a design taxonomy
cards.caption: Players use genre to find games; devs mistake it for rules to follow. Steam tags already replaced the old taxonomy — 'roguelike deckbuilder' is a query, not a tradition. Design for the search, break the canon.

cards.title: Open question with no content to build on
Nintendo eShop roundup promo
Generic beginner gamedev tutorial
An indie game that wins when you stop playing
cards.caption: 'We wanted you to fall asleep' — a game whose success metric is ending your session. In an industry KPI'd on retention, that's not a gimmick, it's a critique in playable form. The anti-engagement genre is quietly forming.

cards.title: Soundtrack devlog recap, no angle
The AI dividend in games is skipping AAA
cards.caption: Big labs chase infrastructure; big studios have pipelines and legal exposure to protect. Indies have neither — so AI leverage compounds where there's no legacy to defend. Tooling revolutions land on the smallest teams first.

cards.title: Game Pass is a variance machine for weird games
cards.caption: A heartfelt sledding sim gets played because subscriptions flatten the price signal — trying a marginal-cost-zero game is free. Weird indie ideas don't need better marketing; they need pricing that removes the decision.

cards.title: When the art is the collision map, clips market themselves
cards.caption: Fresco's Egyptian carvings are the playable surface — 2k likes off one clip. The viral engine isn't polish, it's a verb you can read in a screenshot. If a mechanic needs explaining, the trailer's losing. Caught early in a curated lane.

cards.title: Games are the least-tested software we ship
cards.caption: 'Fun' has no assert statement, so regressions in games get found by vibes, weeks late. Every dev knows this exact feeling — the fix isn't more QA hours, it's replay-based tests that treat old sessions as fixtures.
cards.quote: has always worked suddenly doesn't work anymore and you have no idea for how long it has been broken

cards.title: AI cut the costs that were never the bottleneck
cards.caption: The honest indie pipeline: AI for backgrounds and grunt work, real 3D where it fails. Asset cost collapses; taste doesn't. The constraint just moved from budget to judgment — and judgment doesn't scale with tokens.

cards.title: On a Game Boy cart, the music outweighs the code
cards.caption: An assembly dev's ROM budget: song data is the single biggest line item — bigger than the engine. Audio has been games' heaviest asset since the 80s. Chiptune wasn't an aesthetic choice; it was a compression algorithm with style.

cards.title: Monthly release list, no take
45-minute games are a format, not a flaw
cards.caption: A horror game beaten in 45 minutes, praised for exactly that. Dread doesn't scale — runtime inflation is a AAA pricing pathology, not a design goal. Short horror is games rediscovering the short story.

cards.title: Crafting-game promo with engagement bait
The indie studio is becoming one human plus agents
cards.caption: An AI analyst that watches Steam, Discord and reviews so a solo dev doesn't tab between five dashboards. The signal isn't this product — it's that 'staffing' a small studio now means renting attention, not hiring it.

cards.title: GenAI disclosure can cut a Steam game's reviews 58%
cards.caption: First quantified price of AI backlash: disclosed GenAI assets can cut a game's Steam reviews — a decent sales proxy — by 58%. 'Human-made' is becoming a paid label; organic-food economics, arriving in games.
cards._dedup: same study as card-100 (53% vs 58% framing)

cards.title: Same account and pitch as card-71
Indie games are the new film option market
cards.caption: A Japanese horror film adapted from a short indie game. The pipeline inverted: a 2-hour game is a storyboard with a proven audience attached — cheaper to option than a script, pre-marketed by Let's Plays. Spotted in my horror lane.

cards.title: Stat thread cut off before the payoff number
Audio-first design finds mechanics sighted devs miss
cards.caption: A jam for blind-accessible games. Constraints are generators: designing without the screen forces spatial audio, rhythm and haptic verbs that sighted-first design never discovers. Accessibility is R&D wearing a compliance badge.

cards.title: Retweet of card-78's promo
Weekly releases promo grid, engagement bait
Budget buys polish by sanding off personality
cards.caption: Indies keep out-charting AAA and it isn't romance — it's variance. A $200M budget forces median taste through ten approval layers; a 5-person team can price risk. Personality isn't what survives the budget, it's what budget removes.

cards.title: Generic 2026 gamedev roadmap video
Failure postmortems are the only honest gamedev data
cards.caption: Dev lists 10 design mistakes that hurt his own game. Success postmortems are marketing; failure ones are data — nobody retrofits a narrative onto a flop. Want real base rates? Read the losers. The postmortem lane is the one worth curating.

cards.title: Undertale's code is a mess. It didn't matter.
cards.caption: Undertale's dialogue famously lives in one giant switch statement — and it still rewired the RPG genre. Code quality is a maintenance cost, not a product feature. Players ship with the design, not the repo.
cards.quote: Toby Fox is a bad developer

cards.title: A $39 indie just outsold the $70 crowd
cards.caption: When a $39 indie becomes the best-seller in years, the price anchor moves. AAA pricing was never about value — it was production-cost recovery. Players just got shown the difference in sales-chart form.

cards.title: Proton quietly killed the native Linux port
cards.caption: Gamers mad that indies skip Linux builds miss the mechanism: Proton made native ports economically irrational. Valve's compatibility layer absorbed the very incentive Valve preached — it works so well nobody has to ship for it.

cards.title: Next Fest's top demo: the pitch is the mechanic
cards.caption: Bombanana won Next Fest: three monkeys defusing a bomb — one blind, one mute, one deaf. The one-line premise IS the design doc. Wishlist charts increasingly reward describable games over screenshottable ones. Worth a lane in your reader.

cards.title: Engines commoditized code. Art is the new moat.
cards.caption: An artist shipping a game with 'just art skills' isn't an underdog story anymore. Godot and Unity commoditized the programming; the scarce input flipped. In 2026 gamedev, art direction is the one part you can't download.

cards.title: Reviewing the game, not the developer's drama
cards.caption: A Heartbound design study that skips the Pirate Software discourse entirely. Rare move — games criticism usually can't separate design from developer reputation, which is exactly why most of it tells you nothing about the game.

cards.title: Epic is deprecating Blueprints — and a decade of tutorials
cards.caption: Blueprints and Actors, both deprecated. The real cost isn't migration — it's that ten years of tutorials, courses and marketplace assets just became legacy content overnight. Engine churn is a tax indies pay and never budget for.

cards.title: Why every indie launch post begs for reviews
cards.caption: It's not neediness — Steam hides your review score until 10 reviews land, and the algorithm feeds on that signal. Launch-week review asks are a rational response to a threshold Valve designed. The gratitude is real; so is the gate.

cards.title: The $100 offer isn't for the game
cards.caption: Lowball rights offers aren't buying revenue — they're buying a live Steam app ID, its review history and IP optionality. A shipped store page has a speculative floor value even when the game flopped. Most devs don't know they hold an asset.
cards.quote: An indie dev was just offered $100 for the full rights to their Steam game.

cards.title: AI's gamedev win isn't assets — it's the bug loop
cards.caption: Everyone scrutinizes AI art slop. Meanwhile a dev wires Godot + Claude + Playwright and fixes a player-reported bug live in three minutes. The leverage isn't generating content — it's collapsing maintenance time back into design time.

cards.title: The demo is the ad: asset-pack economics
cards.caption: A pixel artist ships a polished platformer scene to sell the tileset behind it. Asset stores flipped the funnel — the demo exists to market the middleware. A growing share of 'indie games' in your feed are storefronts for their own parts.

cards.title: The AI label costs a Steam game 53% of its reviews
cards.caption: Disclose AI use on Steam and reviews drop ~53% — and skew more negative. The disclosure checkbox became a punishment mechanism, which trains devs to hide usage. A transparency system that prices honesty negatively will produce less of it.

cards.title: 4 years is the real price tag on a solo indie game
cards.caption: Every solo-dev revenue screenshot hides the denominator. Four years of full-time work is the actual cost basis — divide any indie 'success' by that and most wins land near minimum wage. Time math is the least-shared stat in gamedev.

cards.title: When an EA exec says 'creativity,' read 'margin'
cards.caption: EA's Laura Miele credits AI with a rise of creativity across studios. Decode the exec-speak: publishers narrate AI to investors as output-per-headcount. Watch what actually ships, not what gets said on an earnings-adjacent press tour.

cards.title: The Unity exodus question won't die. That's the story.
cards.caption: 'Game Development Post-Unity' still pulls 147 comments on HN. Unity walked back the runtime fee; the migration talk never walked back. Trust is a dependency — no patch release restores it. Engine choice is now risk analysis.

cards.title: A live design autopsy — the patient can still patch
cards.caption: New metroidvania sits at 65% Mixed and gets a public design teardown with proposed fixes. Post-launch criticism used to be an obituary; on Steam it's free QA the dev can ship against. In an oversupplied genre, that's survival advice.

cards.title: 'Bad marketing' is indie dev's most comforting myth
cards.caption: Eastshade's data argues most indies don't fail from marketing. The harsher read: Steam's algorithm IS distribution — it tests every game for conversion. 'I failed at marketing' usually means the store ran the experiment and the game lost.

cards.title: The 'genres that sell' chart invalidates itself
cards.caption: Every dev sees the same Steam genre data, so the green bars attract a flood of supply. Demand-side charts used as supply-side advice self-destruct — by the time a genre tops the list, you're late. The red bars might be the actual arbitrage.

cards.title: Indie codebases are disposable. Designs aren't.
cards.caption: Jonas Tyroller on indie architecture, and the quiet heresy inside it: clean code protects a future most unshipped games never reach. Optimize for iteration speed, treat the repo as scaffolding — the design is the asset, not the codebase.

cards.title: Gamedev advice works better inverted
cards.caption: A whole series that teaches indie dev by how-to-fail. Via negativa beats success advice here: failure modes repeat across projects, success paths don't. You can't copy a hit, but you can reliably stop repeating the known losses.

cards.title: 96% of indie games fail — and the stat is unfalsifiable
cards.caption: Only ~4% of Steam games turn a profit. But the 96% includes jam entries, portfolios and hobby uploads with zero commercial intent. The real base rate for serious attempts is unknowable — which is exactly why the scary number never dies.

cards.title: Don't make smaller games. Make subtracted ones.
cards.caption: Subtractive design through the genre lens: find which 20% of a genre's mechanics carry the fun, cut the inherited rest. Scope discipline isn't shrinking your idea — it's refusing to pay for conventions the genre only kept out of habit.

cards.title: Game design's canon is being written on YouTube
cards.caption: Three hours of design analysis in one video — it will outlive half the games it covers. Design education skipped academia; the canon forms in community-curated essays. A good reader works the same way: someone did the three hours for you.
